coredata.dc:title: Landscaping sustainable conversion of waste plastics to carbon dots and enormous diversity in O2 harvesting, hypoxia, autophagy
coredata.dc:creator: Kommula, Bramhaiah
Banoo, Maqsuma
Roy, Raj Sekhar
Sil, Supriya
Sah, Arjun Kumar
Rawat, Bhawna
Chakraborty, Sagnik
Meena, Pradhyuman
Kailasam, Kamalakannan
Gautam, Ujjal K.
coredata.dc:description: Recent discoveries using polyethylene-derived carbon dots (CD) not only offer tangible ways of mitigating plastics pollution but also open tremendous application prospects. Using their massive aerial-oxygen-harvesting properties, oxidation reactions can be accomplished with equal efficiencies in air and in oxygen, enabling the use of free air as oxidants, while their ability to self-eliminate or ‘autophagy’ enables easy disposal. Light-induced ‘hypoxia’ in them can provide for programmable oxygen concentrations within a solution. However, utilizing different waste plastics as feedstock and the tunability of the emergent properties has remained unexplored. Herein, we show that CDs can be obtained from ten of the most abundant plastic wastes with 100% conversion efficiencies, and recovered as powders for easy transportation and storage. We further establish that O2 harvesting ability and the magnitude of hypoxia in CDs are highly structure-dependent and rationally controllable. O2 enrichment can be tremendously modulated in the as-synthesized CDs within a 405–830 μM range where the ratios of strongly and weakly bound oxygen molecules vary between 1.46 and 3.14. Moreover, hypoxia can be adjusted in the 82–266 μM range with 30 min of light exposure while autophagy follows a pseudo-first-order kinetics with effective disappearances from a solution spanning from two days to months.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aerial oxygen harvesting
Carbon dots
CD-autophagy
CD-Hypoxia
Waste plastic utilization

Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: B.K. is grateful to the IISER Mohali for providing a postdoctoral fellowship, M.B. is grateful to CSIR (India), for the senior research fellowship, R.S.R. is thankful to MHRD, India, for a senior research fellowship (SRF). S.S. thanks to UGC for the junior research fellowship. Support from the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India , under Grant CRG/2021/001420 is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of CDs synthesis using waste plastics via acid-mediated charr followed by chemical oxidative fragmentation (b) various waste plastics used in this study and their chemical structures (c) and (d) high-resolution TEM images of PET CDs, and PVC CDs: top inset shows lattice fringes, and the bottom insets show a bar diagram depicting particle size distribution (TEM images for other plastics waste derived CDs are provided in SI) (e), and (f) deconvoluted C-1s XPS spectra of PP-CDs (low ‘C’ content), and PVC-CDs (high ‘C’ content), respectively, and (g) particle size distribution bar diagram of various CDs.

Optical properties of the PE-CDs, PVC-CDs, and PET-CDs: (a) contour plot of λex dependent PL emission spectra of PE-CDs, PVC-CDs, and PET-CDs, (b) bar diagram depicting PL emission maxima wavelengths, the corresponding PL excitation wavelengths, and PL Quantum yields for the various CDs. (c) PL lifetime plots of Nyl-CDs and PAN-CDs at λex ∼375 nm, (d) longest average lifetimes of various CDs (at λex ∼375 nm), (e) PL emission spectra of PE-CDs at a temperature range of 15–70 °C (heating cycle), and 70-15 °C (cooling cycle), (f) relative variations of PL emission intensities with respect to its intensity at 15 °C at different temperatures for the PE-CDs, and PAN-CDs, and (g) cycling stability of the PL emission plot of PE-CDs at 70 °C, and 15 °C temperatures.

(a) Temporal variation of total O2 content in aqueous PS-CDs solution and (b) in aqueous PC-CDs solution while purging with N2 gas, and subsequently during the spontaneous O2 uptake from the air by the nitrogen saturated CDs solutions, (c) plot showing TO contents in aqueous solutions of the various CDs (O2 adsorption), and total O2 content after the N2 saturation (O2 desorption) called as remnant O2 content here, (d) weakly bound and strongly bound oxygen contents, and their ratios estimated for all the CDs, (e, f) three repeated cycles showing oxygen release by the CDs under N2 atmosphere and subsequent oxygen uptake from the air by PS-CDs, and PC-CDs respectively.

(a) Schematic illustration of light-induced hypoxia in CDs solutions, (b) comparison of temporal variations in the TO contents of an aqueous PC-CDs solution under constant illumination of light for various time periods (5, 10, 20, and 30 min) followed by spontaneous O2 uptake from the air in the dark, and (c) temporal variations in the TO content values of the various CDs solutions under constant illumination of light for 30 min, and followed by spontaneous O2 uptake from the air in the dark; insets show the percentage of TO content change after the light illumination.

(a) Schematic illustration of the CD-autophagy: conversion of CDs into CO2 under natural sunlight, (b, c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of an aqueous solution of PC-CDs and PSU-CDs respectively recorded after various days while keeping it exposed to natural sunlight: inset display the corresponding kinetics plot (absorbance at ∼420 nm), (d) plot of autophagy rate (-k1) vs. PL-QY, and (e) photograph of a CDs solution in dark showing no changes after the 1st day and 17th day, and photographs of various CDs solutions in a beaker kept exposed to sunlight at different days such as 1st, 8th, and 17th days showing gradual disappearance of the CDs from the solution.

head.ce:title: Landscaping sustainable conversion of waste plastics to carbon dots and enormous diversity in O2 harvesting, hypoxia, autophagy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER)-Mohali, Mohali, SAS Nagar, Punjab, 140306, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER)-Mohali

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER)-Mohali, Mohali, SAS Nagar, Punjab 140306, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Functional Nanomaterials, Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST), Knowledge City, Sector-81, Manauli, SAS Nagar, 140306, Mohali, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Functional Nanomaterials, Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST), Knowledge City, Sector-81, Manauli, SAS Nagar, 140306 Mohali, Punjab, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent discoveries using polyethylene-derived carbon dots (CD) not only offer tangible ways of mitigating plastics pollution but also open tremendous application prospects. Using their massive aerial-oxygen-harvesting properties, oxidation reactions can be accomplished with equal efficiencies in air and in oxygen, enabling the use of free air as oxidants, while their ability to self-eliminate or ‘autophagy’ enables easy disposal. Light-induced ‘hypoxia’ in them can provide for programmable oxygen concentrations within a solution. However, utilizing different waste plastics as feedstock and the tunability of the emergent properties has remained unexplored. Herein, we show that CDs can be obtained from ten of the most abundant plastic wastes with 100% conversion efficiencies, and recovered as powders for easy transportation and storage. We further establish that O2 harvesting ability and the magnitude of hypoxia in CDs are highly structure-dependent and rationally controllable. O2 enrichment can be tremendously modulated in the as-synthesized CDs within a 405–830 μM range where the ratios of strongly and weakly bound oxygen molecules vary between 1.46 and 3.14. Moreover, hypoxia can be adjusted in the 82–266 μM range with 30 min of light exposure while autophagy follows a pseudo-first-order kinetics with effective disappearances from a solution spanning from two days to months.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastics are pervasive due to their cheap production and durability. However, used plastics eventually escape into the environment due to their non-biodegradable nature causing pollution and healthcare concerns of monumental proportions. [1–4] The state-of-the-art plastic waste management processes are inadequate, leading to a disparity between waste generation and utilization. [1,3,5] Therefore, findings of tangible alternatives to their remediation or utilization are urgent for sustainability.

Carbon dots (CDs) with unique structural, chemical, and photo-physical properties are an emergent class of carbon nanomaterials for a wide range of applications including optoelectronics, catalysis, sensors, bio-imaging, etc. [6–14] CDs can be easily realized by top-down or bottom-up approaches, making their synthesis extremely convenient in comparison to most functional nanomaterials. [7,15–19] Notwithstanding this, however, the separation of the CDs from the aqueous reaction aliquot poses a serious challenge due to their high solubility, thus making their storage, transport, and use in other reaction media difficult. [14,20–25] Purification of CDs from the reaction media is often achieved by either dialysis or column chromatography. Dialysis requires expensive membranes, large volumes of solvents, and several days even for obtaining small quantities of CDs. Similarly, column chromatography is also solvent-intensive, time-consuming, and low-yielding to enthuse industrial-scale production processes. [20–22,26] In addition, most CDs are usually amorphous and potentially unstable to light exposure due to photo-bleaching. [9,16,17,27,28] In this regard, we recently demonstrated that waste polyethylene can be converted to CDs by oxidative acid treatment with several beneficial features. In the process, H2SO4 treatment converts polyethylene to graphitic carbon containing ample sp
3 defects and when these defects are oxidized using H2O2, the graphitic regions separate out as CDs. [29–32] The conversion of plastic is 100%. These CDs exhibit a narrow size distribution (∼7 ± 3 nm), high crystallinity, and stability under intense sunlight. [29,30] In addition, the CDs can be easily collected as a solid powder and the solvent quantity used for purification is just a tiny fraction of that used in dialysis and column chromatography. The fact that all solvents involved in the process can be recycled makes this process commercially attractive. Therefore, it can be an ideal approach to convert other waste plastics to carbon dots too though success can not be guaranteed due to differences in polymer backbones in different plastics.

Subsequently, three new properties of the polyethylene-generated CDs (PE-CDs) were identified with profound application prospects. [29–31] The PE-CDs dissolved in water adsorb large quantities of molecular oxygen from the air in a reversible manner. These absorbed O2 molecules (>100 molecules per CD), when coupled with the innate photoactivity of the CDs, convert to various reactive oxygen species (ROS) and carry out photocatalytic oxidation reactions with as much efficiency as that achieved otherwise under high oxygen partial pressure. This paves the way for the use of free air for highly efficient photo-oxidation reactions relegating the need for separation of oxygen from the air or the use of unfriendly oxidants such as H2O2 or KMnO4. [29–31] Second, it was found that when other reactants are absent in the reaction medium, the CDs exposed to natural sunlight react with neighboring CDs to eliminate them from the solution in the form of CO2 within a month (called ‘autophagy’), suggesting that they can be safely discarded to the environment with a minimal botheration of accumulation concerns. [30] Finally, a remarkable effect of light on the adsorbed oxygen was discovered, not known for any other material or adsorbed gases, that light induces partial but pronounced desorption of O2 molecules from the CDs in a reversible manner (called ‘hypoxia’), the extent of which can be controlled by using different light wavelength or intensity and thus offering the possibility of creating oxygen gradient inside a reaction medium that can, in effect, be controlled externally. [31] However, it is not yet examined if the autophagy rates and the extent of hypoxia can be controlled in a predetermined manner. In view of these, finding ways to tune autophagy and hypoxia are also critical to potential applications.

The above-mentioned advantages are besides the important fact that it provides an effective way of utilizing waste plastic. Therefore, extending this process from polyethylene to other waste plastics can not only contribute to the industrial-scale preparation of high-quality CDs but can also help tackle the widespread plastic pandemic. [2,3,29,33] The present study demonstrates the facile upcycling possibility for ten of the most stubborn plastic wastes including polypropylene (PP), polystyrene (PS), polyvinylchloride (PVC), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyurethane (PU), polycarbonate (PC), nylon (Nyl), polysulfone (PSU) to obtain CDs using the oxidative acid-treatment strategy (Fig. 1 b). Each transformation is accompanied by 100% conversion of the precursor plastic to form highly crystalline CDs, unlike most literature reports of amorphous CDs. Importantly, we landscape the emerging properties viz. oxygen harvesting capacity, light-induced hypoxia, and autophagy and thereby demonstrating their extreme tunability for the first time and providing a ‘pick and choose’ option for various applications.

In this study, we kept the synthesis conditions for CDs using various waste-plastic feedstocks identical since the functional groups responsible for their interesting properties and potential applications arise during their formation stage (Fig. 1a and b). Initially, waste plastics were dispersed into conc. H2SO4, and heated at ∼150 °C (details in the experimental section, supporting note 1) where the entire plastic mass converted to charr from dehydrogenation. [29,34,35] Note that the charring process is extremely slow at room temperature for all plastics, but at ∼150 °C, it begins within a few minutes accompanied by a visible change in the color of the reaction mass. However, depending on the composition and the thickness of the plastic pieces, and due to varied diffusivity of H2SO4 within the plastic framework, the charring process may take 1–12 h to complete, as evidenced by FTIR, Raman, and DSC experiments (Figs. S1, S2, S3, and Table S1). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman analyses (Figs. S5 and S6) of the different charrs confirm that the black mass is composed of defect-containing graphitic carbon with an expanded lattice along the c-axis due to oxidation of the basal planes. [16,36] In the case of a few plastic precursors, the charr content floats on the surface of the reaction mixture owing to its hydrophobic nature but can be made to disperse within the aqueous media by surface oxidation by adding a KMnO4 solution. Upon this, the controlled addition of a small quantity of H2O2 oxidizes the amorphous carbon portions within the charr. The reaction mixtures instantly turn onto a reddish-yellowish solution containing the well-dispersed CDs that were extracted using ethyl acetate and collected as wet powders by evaporating the solvent. These CDs are highly dispersible and remained stable for months in various polar organic solvents such as alcohols, ethyl acetate, acetone, DMSO, etc. (inset of Fig. S5).

Further, to investigate the crystallinity and morphology of CDs, we performed X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The XRD analyses confirmed that all CDs are crystalline with broad diffraction peaks of 25.9° corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite [29,36] (Figs. S5 and S7). Unlike the charr which was expanded, the (002) peaks of the CDs match that of graphite. Fig. 1c and d and Figs. S8 and 9 show the typical TEM and HRTEM images of the various CDs. All CDs possess a quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of ∼4–7 nm and a size distribution of ± 2–3 nm (bottom inset of Fig. 1c and d, Figs. S8 and 9, Table S1). Note: in the case of PSU as the carbon source, we observed carbon nanorod morphologies along with the dot structures (Fig. S9). The high-resolution TEM images (top insets in Fig. 1c and d, and Figs. S8 and 9) confirm the XRD analysis that the CDs are indeed composed of graphitic regions containing lattice fringes with spacings of 0.22 (±1) nm corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite. [18,37,38] It is important to highlight here that CDs synthesized using molecular carbon precursors in most literature reports usually end up with amorphous carbon structures: either the entire CD possesses an amorphous nature or contains a crystalline core with an amorphous outer shell. [9,15,17,18,39,40] On the other hand, the present strategy produces well-crystalline CDs because of the crystalline carbonization of the plastic precursor followed by its oxidative fragmentation.

Since our CD extraction and purification process is highly simplified as compared to those in the literature, we further examined to find that our CDs obtained by simple solvent extraction and those purified further using column chromatography are identical optically and in microscopy observations. Thus, the quantity of the solvent used in our process and processing times are just a tiny fraction of those in dialysis and column chromatography. Besides, the extracting solvent and the acid used for the carbonization of these plastics can be recycled, making this process commercially attractive. There is another important advantage. Like most soluble CDs in the literature decorated with hydrophilic functional groups that are not possible to separate as a powder, [15,29,30,41] our CDs are also extremely dispersible in aqueous media, and yet, could be separated by simple evaporation of the extracting solvent, invaluable for commercial storage and transportation.

The CDs were, however, found to be hygroscopic in the air. We, therefore, evaluated their adsorbed water contents as a measure of their hydrophilicity using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). In all CDs, four important steps of weight loss were observed (Fig. S10). The first prominent mass loss completes ∼120 °C, suggested to be related to the loss of chemisorbed water. [42,43] It is more pronounced for PE-CDs (∼32.4 ± 2%) but generally in the range of 5–10%. The 2nd step in the decomposition occurred at around 120–300 °C, undergoing a ∼43.6% (±2%) weight loss, attributed to the removal of surface functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, epoxy, and sulfonyl groups. [42,43] The 3rd decomposition step at ∼ 300°-700 °C is related to the elimination of defective carbon structures, and phenyl groups, with a total weight loss of ∼24% (±2%). [42] Finally, the leftover-carbon backbone was eliminated after 700 °C, indicating that CDs possess more than ∼75% (±10%) of adsorbed water molecules and surface functional groups, and only ∼25% (±5%) of the carbon backbone (Fig. S10d). Note that mass loss for adsorbed water may change depending on the air exposure time. In the present analysis, considering the functional groups as part of the CDs, we assume that the mass remaining at 120 °C is the actual mass of the CDs.

To understand the surface functional groups and quantitative estimation of the surface chemical compositions, we carried out detailed FTIR and high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements. The detailed FTIR analysis is given in Supporting note 2 and Fig. S11, showing that all CDs are decorated with -OH, CO, C–O, COOH, C–O–C, –SO3H functional moieties and revealing from a strong CC peak that the CDs are made of graphitic structures. The elemental composition (atomic % from XPS data) of the as-prepared CDs is shown in Table S3. A pronounced medium intense peak was observed in the deconvoluted C-1s core-level XPS spectra for all CDs at ∼284.4 (±0.2) eV, assigned to the –CC graphitic (sp 2) carbons (Fig. 1 e & f, Fig. S12). A high intense peak noticed at ∼284.8 (±0.2) eV corresponds to the C–C carbons (defects or sp 3 hybridized C). Peaks at ∼285.8 (±0.2) eV are related to the -C-O (hydroxyl), and/or -C-O-C (epoxy) groups, and, those at ∼286.7 (±0.2) eV and ∼289.6 (±0.2) eV correspond to the –CO (carbonyl), and –COOH (carboxylic) groups respectively. [38,41,44] We further estimated the relative percentages of the various carbon functionalized groups, and the same has been shown in supporting information (Table S4). PAN-CDs, PU-CDs, PS-CDs, PP-CDs, PET-CDs, PSU-CDs, and PE-CDs exhibited a maximum sp
2 carbon content in the range of ∼21–28%, whereas, Nyl-CDs had the lowest content (∼15.5%), while the same for PVC-CDs, and PC-CDs are ∼19.5% and ∼18.5%, respectively. Likewise, the O-1s spectra were deconvoluted into two distinctive peaks at ∼532.7 (±0.2) eV, and ∼533.8 (±0.2) eV related to the oxygen moieties forming single and double bonds respectively (Fig. S13). [10,29,30,44] The presence of S-based groups in the CDs was established from a strong intense peak in the range of 165–175 eV. The S-2p spectra of the CDs contain two peaks with a stronger one at 169.8 (±0.2) eV, and the other at 170.9 (±0.2) eV corresponding to the sulfonic acid and sulfinates (Fig. S13). [29,45] Overall, the dominant sp 2 carbon (CC) peaks suggest that the CDs are primarily having a graphitic core along with various oxygen functionalities including hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic, and sulfonyl groups over the surface of the CDs. In addition to these, we measured the particle size distribution of as-synthesized various CDs from the HRTEM images and displayed the average particle size of 4–7 (±2) nm (Fig. 1g).

A notable and crucial to the CD synthesis mechanism observation was made from the analysis of the CDs prepared from nitrogen or chlorine-containing waste plastics (PAN, Nylon, and PU-containing nitrogen; PVC-containing chlorine). Although, these had ‘N’ or ‘Cl’ heteroatoms in their polymer backbone, no traces of N or Cl were found in the XPS spectra of the product CDs (Fig. S14). In the case of PVC and PAN, the carbon backbone is similar to that of PE, and hence their carbonization is also expected to be identical, as demonstrated in our earlier study. [29] Therein, any heteroatoms on the polymer side chain should be eliminated during charring, leading to N/Cl-free CDs. [29] On the other hand, PU and Nyl comprise of dissimilar carbon backbones. XPS analysis of these CDs showed that N is missing in them too. This leads to the speculation that the growth mechanism may differ from what was earlier proposed that the C-chains in the polymer, after dehydrogenation, curl to form the graphene domains of a CD. The absence of N indicates that the polymer chain must have been ruptured on either side of the N moieties in the polymer chain, and this would give rise to tiny moieties that must have reacted further to produce the graphitic regions. In that scenario, it is not clear how the small molecules would have survived the harsh synthesis conditions at 150 °C, but since it does, it provides an additional handle to tailor the CDs by exploring the reactivity of these in-situ generated tiny reactants. This hypothesis is also supported by the fact that the chain-oxygen must be removed from PSU, for example, to obtain the charr. Thus, even though the synthesis of CDs appears a top-down approach, it in reality may be a bottom-up approach. A detailed description of the plausible reaction mechanism is given in supporting information as supporting note 3, and Figs. S15 and 16.

Enormously diversified aerial O
2
harvesting, hypoxia, and autophagy by the various CDs: We recently developed a critical application perspective toward the use of ambient air as facile and free oxidants in photocatalytic reactions based on our observations that CDs reversibly absorb large quantities of molecular O2 (∼1 wt% or >100 molecules/CD) from the air at ambient pressure and can convert them to reactive oxygen species (ROS) under light irradiation. In general, oxidation chemistry utilizing molecular O2 from the air is of huge industrial relevance, to avoid using pressurized O2 or toxic oxidizing agents such as KMnO4. Similar to facile ROS generation, autophagy, and hypoxia are also critically dependent on the optoelectronic properties of the CDs. Therefore, the optical properties of the various waste plastic-derived CDs were investigated first as discussed in detail in supporting note 4.

Briefly, the CDs have optical absorption onsets at ∼500–525 nm and multiple absorption bands in the 300–500 nm region, besides tremendous tuneability in photoluminescence (PL) properties (Figs. S17–S20). The presence of multiple excitation centers in them was indicated by multiple peaks in their PL excitation spectra, also confirmed by steady-state PL measurements (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S17b). [16–18,41] For example, emission spectra of PE-CDs and PET-CDs exhibit maximum PL intensities (λPL) at ∼520 nm and ∼430 nm, respectively (Fig. S17c). Systematically plotted PL spectra at different λex (Figs. S19–S20) showed that all CDs exhibited variable luminescence maxima by changing λex, albeit minimally for a few. For the PE-CDs, PP-CDs, PVC-CDs, PC-CDs, and Nyl-CDs, λPL changes considerably and exhibits two strong emissions separated by ∼100 nm by changing λex. PET-CDs, on the other hand, exhibit a nearly λex-independent PL emission. PSU-CDs have two strong luminescence peaks very close to each other and resemble single chromophores, though they may actually have multiple chromophores with overlapping luminescence or the same ones in non-identical environments. [39,46–48] The PL quantum yields (QY) of the various CDs show huge variations in the range of ∼2–15. A comparison of the PL QYs with the PL emission maxima wavelength of the CDs can be seen in Fig. 2b (Supporting note 4; the QY values were measured at an λex that corresponds to the maximum PL intensity of the sample). PAN-CDs possess the highest QY of ∼15%, while the same for PP-CDs is merely ∼2%. In addition, as seen in Fig. 2 c & d, we also observed considerable variations in their average PL lifetime from 1.98 ns (Nyl-CDs) to 3.3 ns (PAN-CDs) and temperature sensitivities of their fluorophores (Fig. 2e, and Fig. S23), potentially utilizable as luminescent probes for efficient measurements of unknown temperatures. The PL emission intensity (F) decreases as the temperatures increase, but the same is regained by decreasing the temperature to its initial value (F0), evidencing the temperature stability of the fluorophores. Fig. 2f presents the plot of F/F0 (ratios of PL intensity at 15 °C, and elevated temperatures) vs. temperature for PE-CDs and PAN-CDs displaying a near-linear relationship. The respective thermal sensitivities are ∼0.45% C−1, and ∼0.84% C−1, matching some of the best responses in the literature (Table S5). The reversibility of the T-dependent PL emission was also found to be near-perfect through multiple thermal cycling (Fig. 2g). Finally, such thermal stability appears more remarkable when coupled with their high photostability PE-CDs, such as the one observed during 100 photocatalytic cycles earlier [29] and 60 min continuous light illumination (Fig. S21). Good photo and thermal stability is expected to improve the scope of using CDs in various environments for aerial oxygen harvesting.

Aerial Oxygen Harvesting : For landscaping the oxygen harvesting properties of the CDs, we systematically investigated them under identical conditions. As discussed earlier that the CDs are hygroscopic and absorb water, so taking a pre-determined quantity of CDs for oxygen measurement may not be error-free. Therefore, we have carried out the measurements using a concentration that leads to identical light absorption at 450 nm. Thus, light absorption normalized oxygen harvesting can be easily reproduced and can also be directly correlated to other photophysical properties.

At first, we attempted to remove all dissolved oxygen from the CD solutions by purging N2 for as long as needed, until a time when no more oxygen could be removed by this process. Then we stopped N2 purging and allowed oxygen to naturally diffuse in from the air. The dissolved oxygen level gradually grew in each solution, initially fast and then slowly until reaching the initial saturation level, thus letting us estimate the desaturated as well as the saturated oxygen levels in the solutions. In that, the rate of increase in the oxygen level provides a sense of oxygen diffusion efficiency in water in the presence of different CDs. Fig. 3 a&b display the temporal variation of the total oxygen (TO) contents in the solution of PS-CDs and PC-CDs demonstrating the diverse ability of oxygen harvesting by different CDs. The dotted horizontal line describes the oxygen level in pure water (∼310 μM) in ambient air. Both the CDs can extract O2 from the air as reflected by the saturation O2 values above the dotted line. However, the ability of the PS-CDs is quite low (only ∼400 μM) while the same by PC-CDs can reach as high as >810 μM, surpassing even PE-CDs (∼630 μM) reported by us previously. [29,30] Moreover, oxygen diffusion in PC-CDs is also very high, reaching the O2 level of water by the deoxygenated solution within 10 min, while the same requires more than 45 min in the case of PS-CDs. The strong affinity of the PC-CDs is further evidenced by the fact that even after prolonged N2 purging, the solution oxygen concentration does not fall below 130 μM, while in the case of PS-CDs, nearly all O2 molecules can be expelled (remanent O2 level is only ∼25 μM).

The variations in the oxygen contents in the solution of CDs (Fig. S24) under different circumstances are deemed to depend on several complex and interdependent factors such as the extent of conjugated domains, defects density in the graphitic domains as well as functional groups on the surfaces, ratiometric proportions of aromatic with amorphous domains, etc. This is outside the purview of the current work to examine the origin and the controlling factors, but these experiments do establish that there are oxygen moieties that are weakly bound as well as strongly bound (to the extent that N2 purging can't displace them from the surface). We, therefore, calculated the ratio of such weakly bound and strongly bound oxygen molecules as shown in Fig. 3d (Supporting Note 5). The ratio can vary from as high as ∼3.11 to as low as ∼1.36, demonstrating the prospects of tailoring the nature of bound oxygen independently by rational precursor design. Also, we note that these values being mere ratios are intrinsic to the material and independent of the actual CD concentration, which is difficult to assess accurately at present due to water absorption, as discussed earlier.

Furthermore, we examined the cycling stability of the PS-CDs and PC-CDs solutions for the oxygen enrichment ability from the air (akin to PE-CDs) [29,30] as displayed in Fig. 3e&f. As seen, once the N2 gas flow is stopped, oxygen diffuses again from the ambient air into the solution to attain the original saturation values during 3 repeat cycles. An insignificant variance in the kinetics of O2 desorption under N2 purging and O2 uptake from the air has been observed, which specifies that the as-synthesized CDs are stable in the repeated cycles, and the oxygen enrichment is reversible, ferrying between the high adsorption O2 levels in the air, and remanent O2 levels under the N2 gas purging. Furthermore, we listed various nanomaterials' molecular O2 harvesting performances from the ambient air based on the available literature, and the same has been provided in the supporting information (Table S6).

Light-Induced Hypoxia : Under light irradiation, the adsorption-desorption equilibrium of O2 molecules may vividly change on a CD surface due to the generation of hot charge carriers that results in a change in its electronic surfaces. However, the effect of light on the O2 concentration (or any other reactant) on a catalyst surface was not explored, hindering the development of photocatalysts that can use ambient air as an oxidizing agent. We recently observed that the amount of molecular O2 adsorbed on PE-CDs can be controlled by light irradiation (light-induced hypoxia) where light acts as a non-invasive control because both its light intensity and wavelength can work independently to adjust the TO contents in the CDs. [31] The observation not only established a pronounced effect of light on reactant concentration near the catalyst active sites but also offers the possibility of creating a landscape of oxygen-rich and oxygen-poor regions within the reaction vessel simply by illuminating it with different kinds of torch lights simultaneously. In the present investigation, we have systematically investigated all CDs prepared from different waste plastics to establish that hypoxia in CDs is greatly tuneable, just like their oxygen harvesting abilities.

Fig. 4 a shows the schematic illustration of the oxygen uptake from the ambient air by the CD solutions and light-induced hypoxia. The oxygen level gradually decreases under the light until it reaches an equilibrium of light-induced desorption vs. readsorption from the surrounding solvent. Fig. 4b shows that at 5 min of continuous illumination, the TO content for the PC-CDs decreases from ∼830 μM (±5%) to ∼799 μM. Once the light illumination ceases, the total O2 content again raises to its initial value. Further on 10 min, 20 min, and 30 min of light illumination, the TO content decreases even further to ∼768, ∼692, ∼and 585 μM, respectively, and after the light illumination ceases, the solution gradually attains its initial saturation values (Fig. 4b). The variation of TO content with the light illumination time has been plotted in Fig. S25 revealing that the decrease in O2 content with illumination time follows a linear progression within this period. However, the TO-decrease becomes less and less with time, reaching equilibrium after 250 min (Fig. S26). Note that the same measurement when performed in pure water, where the TO level remains the same (∼310 μM) under dark and light illumination.

Fig. 4c displays the temporal variation of TO content in the solution of various CDs under continuous light illumination for a fixed 30 min, demonstrating the diverse extent of light induce-hypoxia. While all CDs display a variation of O2 values, the capability of the PP-CDs is comparatively very low, and the total TO content change is only ∼82 μM (±5%). It also takes 120 min to reach its initial TO level in the light-off condition. Nyl-CDs, PS-CDs, PC-CDs, and PET-CDs display a higher alteration of TO content (∼193, ∼266, ∼251, and ∼249 μM respectively, Fig. S27) under identical light illuminations. After the light illumination ceased, it further took ∼120, ∼110, ∼80, and ∼34 min, respectively, for them to attain an initial TO value. The PE-CDs, PVC-CDs, PAN-CDs, and PSU-CDs, on the other hand, exhibited a relatively moderate ∼102, ∼155, ∼171, and ∼110 μM (±5%) decrease in TO under light, and took ∼40, ∼34, ∼140, and ∼120 min to reach their initial TO value, respectively, after the light was switched off. We have so far failed to see a systematic trend in the TO values in the dark and the extent of its lowering under light, which is not surprising though. For example, PE-CDs have a TO value of ∼620 μM in the dark that reduces by just 100 μM under light, while PS-CD's TO value of only ∼400 μM in the dark reduces by ∼250 μM after the same illumination time. It may be pointed out that the O2 adsorption in dark is a ground-state phenomenon for the CD electronic structure, while hypoxia is related to their light-excited states. Also, that O2 adsorption on CD is dependent on the molecular level structure i.e. the various defects, the functional groups, their ratiometric proportions, and also their relative spatial orientations. The diverse nature of the adsorbing sites and their relative abundances is already evident from the uncorrelated TO and RO values. In such a complex scenario, an accurate estimation of the factors overwhelmingly affecting oxygen desorption under light is rather complex and unfathomable at present.

Autophagy : Despite an inimitable range of fascinating properties and potential applications, the recovery of the used CDs from a solution remains a great challenge due to their high solubility, a process much more tedious than their synthesis, which leaves their disposal to the environment as a naturally viable option. In this context, we recently demonstrated a promising phenomenon that PE-CDs can get eliminated from the environment by a process of self-oxidation using sunlight and its own harvested O2 molecules by forming simple molecules such as CO2 (Fig. 5 a). [30] Importantly, the self-oxidation kinetics is slow, taking up to several weeks, and therefore gets suppressed when these CDs are conducting a more facile oxidation reaction such as those industrially important ones. This ‘self-elimination’, when not in use was called CD-autophagy and deemed to be important because it can ensure that the CDs, once disposed of carelessly, may not get accumulated in natural water bodies. In the present investigation, we have explored CD-autophagy for various samples.

Fig. 5b&c display the UV–Vis absorption spectra recorded at various time intervals for the PC-CDs and PSU-CDs solutions. From the plots, it is evident that the absorption of CDs gradually decreases, as the number of days increases under sunlight exposure. This is akin to widely observed bleaching in CDs, but with the added important information that the same is much slower while conducting photocatalysis and eventually leads to their elimination which is beneficial. The absorption spectra of other CDs and the plot of ln (C/C0) vs. time are displayed in Fig. S28. PC-CDs, PE-CDs, PAN-CDs, and PU-CDs exhibit rather slow autophagy rates, while the same is moderate for PVC-CDs, PET-CDs, and PS-CDs. On the other hand, PSU-CDs, Nyl-CDs, and PP-CDs exhibited a much faster degradation (Table S7).

Interestingly for all CDs, we observed two distinct kinetics regimes. Referring to insets in Fig. 5b&c, the PC-CDs exhibited an initial kinetics regime up to 13 days with a rate constant of ∼0.214 day−1, considering first-order kinetics. In the case of PSU-CDs, it took 7 days, and the rate is around 0.438 day−1. We hypothesize that the CD-autophagy process involves two consecutive steps: (i) rapid removal of the surface chromophores that may be easily assessed by in-situ generated ROS, and then (ii) a slow degradation of the rest of the chromophores that are probably buried in the carbon matrix and poorly assessable to ROS. We, however, reckon that there may be alternatives to this simplistic visualization of the autophagy mechanism such as the presence of fluorophores with vastly different reactivities, though it appears less likely considering the harsh synthesis conditions that provide an instant and uniform surrounding to each growing CD.

Fig. 5d shows solutions of all the CDs kept together under natural sunlight for 17 days, where their relative rates of autophagy can be easily visualized. The color of the aqueous solution gradually faded with each passing day and became nearly colorless towards the end. On the other hand, no color change was observed for a beaker containing a solution of CDs kept in dark. Further, the water contents of the beakers were allowed to be evaporated to observe that the CDs disappeared from the beaker exposed to sunlight, while, those in the beaker kept in the dark could be fully recovered. It is also clear that a complete elimination for many CDs (e.g. PE-CDs, PP-CDs, etc.) would require many more days, given that the solutions are still colored after 17 days and that autophagy becomes slow towards the end.

Considering autophagy as a photocatalytic reaction, we explored correlations between the autophagy kinetics and the photoluminescence properties of the respective CDs. We plotted autophagy rate constants -k1 versus their PL-QYs, which revealed that as the PL-QY increases from 2 to 15, the -k1 decreases from ∼0.607 day−1 (PP-CDs) to ∼0.120 day−1 (PAN-CDs, Fig. 5e). This is interesting because pseudo-first-order kinetics further confirms the large molecular O2 concentration on their surfaces. Besides, the observation corroborates well with many studies showing low QY materials exhibiting high photocatalytic activities. [48–50]

In conclusion, we show that ten of the most abundant waste plastics can be converted to carbon dots with 100% efficiency so that they can be easily isolated using a facile, extremely simplified approach to obtain powders of the crystalline CDs for easy storage or transportation. The study firmly establishes the scope of utilizing diverse waste plastics as feed-stocks for carbon-dot synthesis as remediation to plastic pollution. We show that during synthesis, the polymer backbone first ruptures to form smaller fragments, thus offering additional prospects of tailoring their properties. Importantly, we investigated their emergent properties of O2 harvesting (from the air), hypoxia, and autophagy to establish that they are highly tuneable depending on the precursor plastic molecular structure and therefore provide a way of rationally controlling them as per application needs. The data provided here on these properties would serve as a platform for benchmarking, further developments, and finding new avenues for their utilization.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Bramhaiah Kommula: Data curation, Validation, Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Ujjal K. Gautam: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

S.S. thanks to UGC for the junior research fellowship. Support from the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India, under Grant CRG/2021/001420 is gratefully acknowledged.

J. Phys. Chem. C

C. 125 (2021) 4299-4309. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jpcc.1c00004.
